items.title: Inside Banana RAT: From Build Server to Banking Fraud
items.description: An MDR investigation successfully mapped the complete operational infrastructure of Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan operated by threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063. The investigation uncovered both server-side and client-side components, revealing a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system that produces hash-unique builds to evade detection. The malware employs layered
items.ai_summary: The Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan, has been found to employ a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system, producing hash-unique builds to evade detection. This malware is operated by the threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063 and has been linked to banking fraud. The RAT uses layered defenses and can impair system defenses through T1562 tactics, making it challenging to detect and mitigate. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, focusing on unusual traffic patterns and inspecting for signs of T1190 exploit attempts against public-facing applications. Additionally, ensuring all security software is up-to-date and configuring systems to restrict unnecessary outbound connections can help mitigate the threat.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest PyPi Compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client compromised versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3 on PyPi. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account previously linked to attacks, using stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens. The evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Vault,
items.ai_summary: The latest PyPi compromise is a supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client, specifically versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account, utilizing stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens, which is an example of T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise). This evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from various cloud services including AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and Vault, resulting in significant real-world impact for organizations relying on these services. To remediate, defenders should immediately update the Microsoft DurableTask Python client to a version outside the affected range (1.4.1-1.4.3) and review GitHub account security, ensuring that all credentials are secure and that two-factor authentication is enabled. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to the stolen credentials is crucial, such as unusual login attempts from unfamiliar locations or devices.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1195| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Gamaredon campaign targeting Ukraine weaponizing CVE-2025-8088
items.description: A campaign exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability CVE-2025-8088 has been actively targeting Ukraine since February 2026, with ongoing activity through June 2026. The operation uses Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures, distributed as RAR archives. The malicious archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences that automatically place L
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon campaign has been exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability, leveraging Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures to target Ukraine since February 2026. The malicious RAR archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences, which automatically place malware on the victim's system, utilizing T1204 user execution and T1564.004 NTFS file attributes. If exploited, this vulnerability allows attackers to encrypt data for impact, as seen in T1486, and potentially collect sensitive information, as indicated by T1560 archive collected data. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are using the latest version of WinRAR, and implement robust email filtering to block malicious RAR archives, as well as monitor for suspicious NTFS alternate data streams. Additionally, defenders should prioritize user education to prevent user execution of malicious files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1564.004| C[NTFS File Attributes]:::tactic
    C -->|T1560| D[Archive Collected Data]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OXLOADER: new loader evading detection to drop infostealer
items.description: A previously undocumented Windows loader designated as OXLOADER delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving remarkably low detection rates. The loader employs multiple obfuscation layers including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques, along with self-modifying decryption stubs and abuse of the Windows .relo
items.ai_summary: The OXLOADER is a previously undocumented Windows loader that delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving low detection rates due to its multiple obfuscation layers, including control-flow flattening and opaque predicates. This loader affects Windows systems, with the exact version not specified, and can lead to significant real-world impact, such as data breaches and financial loss, as the CASTLESTEALER infostealer can steal sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust anti-malware solutions that can detect and block obfuscated files and information, and ensure that their systems are configured to prevent the abuse of the Windows .relo feature. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on Google Ads and verify the legitimacy of the ads before interacting with them. The threat actor behind OXLOADER is unknown, but their use of advanced obfuscation techniques suggests a high level of sophistication.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Data Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation FlutterBridge: The FlutterShell macOS Backdoor
items.description: FlutterShell is a macOS backdoor campaign active from December 2025 to March 2026, identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089 under Operation FlutterBridge. The threat actors deliberately misused the Flutter framework to deliver malware through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube. The malware employs a two-component architecture: a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib. Across thr
items.ai_summary: The Operation FlutterBridge campaign, active from December 2025 to March 2026, involves the FlutterShell macOS backdoor, which is delivered through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube, exploiting user interactions to compromise systems. This campaign is identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089 and utilizes a two-component architecture consisting of a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib, allowing for remote access and control. The threat actors behind this campaign leverage T1583.008 (Malvertising) and T1189 (Drive-by Compromise) to gain initial access, followed by the use of T1219 (Remote Access Software) for persistence and further exploitation. To remediate, defenders should block malvertising campaigns, implement robust ad-blocking mechanisms, and regularly inspect system logs for signs of unauthorized access, specifically focusing on unusual Mach-O launcher and dylib activities. Furthermore, ensuring all software and operating systems are up-to-date, particularly focusing on security patches related to web browsers and their components, is crucial.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popa: From Sourcing to Distribution
items.description: An Android proxyware SDK named Popa enrolls consumer devices including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes into a commercial residential proxy network. Operating since at least 2020, Popa and its variants (Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser) are distributed inside consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications. The SDK begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch without displaying info
items.ai_summary: The Popa Android proxyware SDK poses a significant threat to consumer devices, including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes, by enrolling them into a commercial residential proxy network without user consent. This SDK, along with its variants Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser, has been operating since at least 2020 and is distributed through various consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications. Once installed, the SDK begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch, potentially leading to data breaches, privacy violations, and other malicious activities. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict app vetting processes, monitor network traffic for suspicious activity, and ensure that all devices have up-to-date security software installed. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing new apps, especially those from unknown sources, and review app permissions carefully to prevent unauthorized access to their devices.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1404| C[Hide In Plain Sight]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1532| E[Data Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Poisson – Analyzing a Cybercriminal’s Entire Operation
items.description: A comprehensive analysis of 339 commands issued by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days, targeting a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker utilized a multi-stage fileless attack deploying a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure including Havoc C2 framework,
items.ai_summary: The Operation Poisson threat involves a French-speaking threat actor utilizing a multi-stage fileless attack to target a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker deployed a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials, leveraging the Havoc C2 framework and free-tier infrastructure. The attack vector involved input capture (T1056) and application layer protocol (T1071) tactics, with a focus on multi-stage channels (T1104) to maintain persistence. To remediate, defenders should implement robust input validation and monitoring, restrict access to sensitive applications, and regularly inspect network traffic for suspicious activity. Specifically, defenders should update their security information and event management (SIEM) systems to detect and alert on potential keylogger activity, and ensure all employees are using two-factor authentication for email and banking services.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1056| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular node-ipc npm Package Infected with Credential Stealer
items.description: A supply chain attack has compromised the node-ipc npm package, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. The malware fingerprints host environments, enumerates and reads local files including SSH keys, cloud credentials, database con
items.ai_summary: The node-ipc npm package has been compromised in a supply chain attack, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated credential stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved the takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain, allowing the threat actor to inject malware into the package. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to the theft of sensitive credentials, including SSH keys, cloud credentials, and database connections, affecting developers and organizations that have installed the compromised package. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the affected versions and update to a safe version, such as 10.0.0 or later, and monitor their environments for signs of malicious activity, including unusual network traffic or file access patterns. Additionally, developers should review their dependency chains and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date and from trusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1592| C[Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078.004| E[Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond Tax Returns: How Shared Malware Infrastructure Scales Brand Abuse In Indonesia
items.description: A sophisticated fraud campaign exploiting Indonesia's tax season targeted 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The GoldFactory threat cluster orchestrated operations using Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families with shared infrastructure abusing over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The a
items.ai_summary: The GoldFactory threat cluster has been exploiting Indonesia's tax season by targeting 67 million residents with fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering, leveraging the T1566 phishing tactic to gain initial access. The attackers utilized the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families, which were executed through user interaction as described in T1204, to compromise victim devices. If exploited, victims may experience financial fraud and identity theft, with the attackers potentially gaining access to sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email and WhatsApp filtering, and educate users on identifying phishing attempts, specifically warning them against interacting with suspicious Coretax applications. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual network activity and implement endpoint detection and response tools to identify and block the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Dragon Whistle: UNG002 Targets Chinese Academia via Weaponized Institutional Lure
items.description: A sophisticated spear-phishing campaign designated Operation Dragon Whistle has been identified targeting Changzhou University in China. The threat actor UNG002 leveraged highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications regarding mandatory 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which directly impacts graduation eligibility. The
items.ai_summary: The Operation Dragon Whistle campaign, attributed to threat actor UNG002, employs sophisticated spear-phishing tactics to target Changzhou University in China. By impersonating official university communications regarding the 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, UNG002 leverages highly contextual social engineering to trick victims into executing malicious code, potentially impacting graduation eligibility. The attack vector involves phishing emails (T1566) that rely on user execution (T1204) to initiate the exploit. To defend against this threat, institutions should implement robust email filtering and employee education programs, focusing on verifying the authenticity of emails, especially those that create a sense of urgency or importance. Specific remediation steps include configuring email clients to flag emails with spoofed sender addresses and training staff to recognize and report suspicious communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-3326: The Xstore WordPress theme before 9.7.3 does not properly sanitise and escape a ...
items.description: The Xstore WordPress theme before 9.7.3 does not properly sanitise and escape a parameter before using it in a SQL statement via an AJAX action available to unauthenticated users, leading to a SQL injection
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-3326 is a SQL injection vulnerability in the Xstore WordPress theme, affecting versions prior to 9.7.3. The vulnerability arises from inadequate sanitization and escaping of a parameter used in a SQL statement via an AJAX action, which is accessible to unauthenticated users. This allows an attacker to inject malicious SQL code, potentially leading to unauthorized data access, modification, or deletion. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Xstore WordPress theme version 9.7.3 or later, and ensure that all plugins and themes are up-to-date. Additionally, defenders can implement Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) to detect and prevent SQL injection attacks, and regularly monitor database logs for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Unauthorized Data Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078| E[Data Modification or Deletion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

allow an unauthentic...
items.description:
A vulnerability has been found in some Dahua products could
allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to send a specially crafted packet,
triggering an exception that causes the system to reboot unexpectedly,
resulting in a denial of service.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-29116 is a vulnerability in certain Dahua products that allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to send a specially crafted packet, triggering an exception that causes the system to reboot unexpectedly, resulting in a denial of service. The attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by TTP T1190, and can lead to network denial of service, as seen in T1498. If exploited, this vulnerability can cause significant disruptions to affected systems, potentially impacting surveillance and security operations. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should ensure that their Dahua products are updated to the latest firmware version, and consider implementing network segmentation and traffic filtering to limit the attack surface. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for signs of unexpected reboots or network disruptions, which could indicate a potential exploit attempt.

items.description: A vulnerability has been found in some Dahua products could allow an authenticated remote attacker to send a specially crafted packet, triggering an exception that causes the system to reboot unexpectedly, resulting in a denial of service.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-29115 is a vulnerability in certain Dahua products that allows an authenticated remote attacker to send a specially crafted packet, triggering an exception that causes the system to reboot unexpectedly, resulting in a denial of service. The attack vector involves exploiting the vulnerability through a network interface, potentially affecting Dahua products with outdated firmware. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant disruptions in surveillance and security systems, particularly in industries that rely heavily on these products, such as law enforcement and retail. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update their Dahua products to the latest firmware version, ensuring that all devices are running with the most current security patches. Specifically, users should check for firmware updates on the Dahua website and apply the updates as soon as possible to prevent potential exploitation.

items.title:
CVE-2026-29114: A vulnerability has been found in some Dahua products. An attacker
may obtain th...
items.description:
A vulnerability has been found in some Dahua products. An attacker
may obtain the device’s CA root certificate. If that CA is installed and
trusted on client systems, the attacker could issue fraudulent certificates
trusted by those clients and undermine the certificate trust chain.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-29114 is a vulnerability in certain Dahua products that allows an attacker to obtain the device's CA root certificate. If the CA is installed and trusted on client systems, the attacker can issue fraudulent certificates that are trusted by those clients, undermining the certificate trust chain. This vulnerability affects Dahua products, specifically those with outdated firmware versions, and can be exploited via an unspecified attack vector. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update their Dahua products to the latest firmware version and ensure that the CA root certificate is not installed on client systems. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious certificate activity and implement a certificate validation process to prevent fraudulent certificates from being trusted.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1552| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Remote Services]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-11815: An attacker who intercepts and tampers with traffic between the client applicati...
items.description: An attacker who intercepts and tampers with traffic between the client application and the API Gateway server could potentially deserialize arbitrary objects. This vulnerability could lead to broken security expectations or remote code execution.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11815 is a vulnerability in the API Gateway server that allows an attacker to deserialize arbitrary objects by intercepting and tampering with traffic between the client application and the server. This could lead to broken security expectations or remote code execution, affecting users of the client application. The attack vector involves exploiting the vulnerability in the API Gateway server, potentially allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code or commands on the server. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update their API Gateway server to the latest version, which includes patches for CVE-2026-11815, and ensure that all client applications are configured to use secure communication protocols, such as HTTPS, to prevent tampering with traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor their network traffic for signs of exploitation and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-11837: A local privilege escalation vulnerability was found in the ansible.posix author...
items.description: A local privilege escalation vulnerability was found in the ansible.posix authorized_key module. The module's keyfile() function uses os.chown() instead of os.lchown() and opens files without O_NOFOLLOW when managing SSH authorized keys. An unprivileged local user can pre-stage symbolic links in their ~/.ssh directory to redirect file ownership changes to arbitrary system paths when an operator ru

This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with Contributor-level access and above, to inject ar

items.title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
items.description: Market intelligence platform Klue has publicly confirmed a recent security incident that allowed threat actors to steal OAuth tokens used to connect to customers' Salesforce environments, as the new "Icarus" extortion group publicly claims the attack. [...]

items.title: Hackers exploit info disclosure bug in Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting an unauthenticated information disclosure vulnerability in the WordPress plugin Gravity SMTP, active on 100,000 sites. [...]

items.title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
items.description: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift have published a working exploit, dubbed usbliter8, that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips. That code is burned into the silicon at manufacture. No software update can reach it. Affected devices will carry this flaw for as long as they stay in use. This is not a remote attack. It requires

items.title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) operation is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers that it hands out to affiliates for impairing system defenses before deploying the encryptor. This mature portfolio of EDR-terminating tools is centered around a framework that's known as GentleKiller. "They also incorporate third-party or

items.title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
items.description: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department (TPWD) disclosed a data breach at its license system vendor that exposed personal information for more than three million individuals. [...]

items.title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
items.description: Microsoft researchers have detailed an exploit chain, named AutoJack, that turns an AI browsing agent into a delivery vehicle for remote code execution. Steer the agent to load an attacker's web page, and that page's JavaScript can reach a privileged local service on the same machine and spawn a process on the host. No credentials, no sign-in screen, and no further user interaction once

items.title: In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Android TV botnet Popa linked to Israeli firm, Velvet Ant maintained decade-long stealth, unpatched GCP Config Connector flaw enables takeover. The post In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
items.description: Dutch law enforcement authorities, along with counterparts from Canada , Germany, and the U.S., have disrupted malicious infrastructure associated with SocGholish and cleaned up nearly 15,000 infected WordPress websites. "With these actions we deprive cybercriminals of access to infected computer systems," Maikel Rollman of the Netherlands National High Tech Crime Unit said. "This prevents

items.title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday urged Fortinet customers with FortiGate appliances to take steps to secure against ongoing malicious activity aimed at thousands of internet-accessible devices. The sweeping campaign, believed to be the work of Russian-speaking threat actors, has been codenamed FortiBleed. The number of compromised devices stands at

items.title: Every AI Agent Is an Identity. Most Organizations Don't Treat Them That Way
items.description: AI agents can access data, trigger workflows, deploy code, and interact with critical business systems, often with little oversight. Token Security breaks down why AI agents are becoming a new identity and governance challenge. [...]

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.

items.title: Webinar: How attackers bypass MFA and how defenders can respond
items.description: Modern phishing attacks, including Device Code phishing, can undermine MFA protections and grant attackers access to corporate accounts without stealing passwords. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help security teams detect compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: From Assistive to Agentic: The AI Shift That's Redefining Threat Management
items.description: Introduction The average enterprise security team has 40 or more security tools, giving a lot of visibility into internal telemetry and asset data. But often, these tools are working in siloes, generating (overlapping) alerts and data. And yet, breach dwell times remain stubbornly long (~43 days), response windows keep closing before teams can act, and analysts burn out triaging noise instead

items.title: Microsoft: June 2026 Windows updates break Recycle Bin prompts
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed a confusing Windows bug that causes different filenames to appear in the confirmation dialog when deleting a file from the Recycle Bin. [...]

items.title: CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor
items.description: CryptoBandits uses a local SOCKS5 proxy for traffic routing, blending data theft with remote code execution. The post CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
items.description: The large-scale credential theft campaign hit roughly half of the internet-accessible Fortinet firewalls and VPNs. The post FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday
items.description: CISA has urged U.S. federal agencies to secure their systems by Sunday against a critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability that is being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: Forget Data Leakage: Shadow AI's Real Threat Is Access Control
items.description: The first wave of enterprise AI concern was straightforward. It was simply employees pasting sensitive data into public AI tools. Security teams responded with usage policies, domain blocks, and data loss prevention rules. That response made sense at the time. It doesn't fit the problem anymore. Shadow AI has shifted from a data leakage concern to an access control problem. The threat isn't

items.title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
items.description: The hackers exfiltrated data from Salesforce instances of Klue customers, such as Huntress and Recorded Future. The post Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
items.description: Salesforce has revealed that it disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration within its platform in response to a security incident impacting the competitive intelligence company on June 11, 2026. To that end, organizations will be unable to connect to Salesforce via the app until further notice, the American cloud-based software company noted in an alert published this week. "Salesforce took

items.title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
items.description: A New York man faces cyberstalking charges after allegedly sharing AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages using fake social media profiles to harass a Georgia college student. [...]

items.title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
items.description: WideField will accelerate Agentic SOC capabilities by expanding the lens on threat investigation to include identity, credentials, sessions, and blast radius. The post Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after nearly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a data leak dubbed "FortiBleed." [...]

items.title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
items.description: Law enforcement and private partners took down 106 SocGholish C&C servers and domains as part of Operation Endgame. The post 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
items.description: Apple has updated its Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds to patch a high-severity vulnerability that could be exploited by nearby hackers to eavesdrop on users. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS score: 8.8), refers to a case of incorrect authorization impacting the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that makes it possible to pair a Bluetooth audio device without user consent.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
items.description: CISA has given federal agencies only three days to patch CVE-2026-20253, which can be exploited for unauthenticated remote code execution. The post Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers to help affiliates evade detection in attacks. [...]

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
items.description: F5 has released security updates to address two critical security flaws in NGINX Open Source that could be exploited to achieve code execution on affected systems. The vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-42530 (CVSS v4 score: 9.2) - A use-after-free vulnerability in the ngx_http_v3_module that could be triggered by a remote unauthenticated attacker when NGINX Open Source is

items.title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
items.description: These servers are regularly targeted by China-linked UNC6508 for initial access and backdoor deployment. The post Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
items.description: The deal values industrial cybersecurity giant Dragos at $3.25 billion, and runZero and NetRise will operate under Dragos. The post Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
